sentences.text: OK, on the road we go out in the field, on the land, and grab potatoes, beans, chicken.
My wife didn't want to eat because I couldn't kill the chicken.
But I had the guy what [LAUGHS] killed them.
And she didn't want to each because the guy killed not kosher.
OK, we cooked and we feed everybody.
Now, I came in Chemnitz, and I came in in a bakery.
And he want to give me only one bread and I need so many people to feed.
I-- I took-- I took something.
I says, if you not going to let me take the bread, I kill you right here.
So he was afraid from me.
And I went and took all the bread out from the back, from the hallway and brought the bread and give it to everybody to eat.
And we stayed in the forest everyday.
We couldn't go further.
The nighttime we would go further.
And we went to a town, Wlodawa, and nobody there was nothing.
They didn't know it's a war.
They didn't know it.
So, well, I had a horse and wagon.
I left the horse on the field, and I went with the train.
Took my family and went to Kovel, to Lutsk.
And in Lutsk they didn't know it's a war either.
So I went to a guy, a chauffeur.
And his truck, he had a brand new truck that was shipped from Kiev.
And we go.
He want to make a road Kiev-Chelm.
So we're going to make [INAUDIBLE].
And I worked for him until May.
My family, we took an apartment
No, not my brother and not my sister, just my family.
My sister was with me in Brest Litovsk.
And my brother went back to Warsaw.
So she says if the brother went back, she goes back, too.
And she will come.
She will pick up some clothes and come back but she never come back.
She lived with my brother in Warsaw.
He had a factory for buggies for kids.
Buggies for kids.
And she lived with him.
And her husband was with me.
So he says, she went back, I go back.
He went back, too.
When the Bug this River Bug was frozen so they can walk over even back then in wintertime.
And I says I'm not going back because it's cold, and I wouldn't have with what to eat.
I'll stay here until summer.
Until summer, I couldn't go back.
Then I was working for that guy, and one day, the Russian picks up all of them and put them in the train.
They didn't took me, but I told my wife, go in the garage.
Sit in the garage because this night they going to pick up everybody.
My wife's sister was with her and her husband.
The husband told my wife, let's go be with everybody.
Let's go with us.
We're not going in the garage.
We stay here.
OK, I was going with two soldiers and picked up the people what didn't belong in Lutsk and put them in the train.
Just was Jews.
There was no other ones.
Well, yeah, I'll tell you where we go.
So I told my wife to stay in the garage.
And I was to pick up the people, bring to the train and go again and go again.
I came in the morning, 6 o'clock in the morning and Maria is in the train and hollered, Da, Da, Da, Da.
Yeah, my wife was already, [LAUGHS] they picked up my wife, too.
It was not me but five, six trucks.
They picked up my wife with the kids.
Yeah, I came there, and Maria hollered, Da, Da, Da, Da.
So I called that guy, that officer, and told him, I'm going, too in that one.
He says, you not going.
You stay with me.
I says, my wife is in there in the train.
I'm going to stay with you, take it out, my wife with the kids and then I'll stay.
He says, I can't do that.
I can't.
How you go?
No, write, and they should send her back.
I can write to them where they're going to take her.
They should send her back.
I says, then you send for me, too.
I go there.
I go in the train.
And I went back, and I brought bread and whiskey.
And I took some clothes, and I went right back to the train.
And I went in the train.
And they took us, was four weeks on the train to Siberia.
Yeah, my wife's sister and her husband, my wife.
And a lot of people, a full train.
It was four weeks until Siberia.
And in Siberia, we started working again.
Until the war broke out.
When the war with the Germans broke out, they released us.
Yeah.
Was it-- no it was--
[NON-ENGLISH]
In Siberia we worked in forest.
No, families together.
It was like a, they built like a shed, like a hotel, rooms.
We had our own room.
My sister-in-law with her husband, had their own room.
I had the own room.
We was four, we had a bigger room.
And we worked in the forest.
No.
Yeah, just took you.
And we went there until the war broke out, Germans with the Russians.
Then they came and the SS, you already free.
Go wherever you want.
Yeah, like a camp.
It was a camp.
They kept their prisoners before.
So then when they took away the prisoners and let us live.
They supposed to give you money, yeah.
Very hard.
Well, we sold what we had.
We sold and we bought food.
We didn't have to buy it.
They cooked one meal everyday.
Supposed to buy it but nobody paid for them.
What?
It was forest, wood, a bunch of wood.
Yeah.
Yeah.
[INAUDIBLE]
Maria was cooking coffee.
[LAUGHS] Maria was a home.
And they want my wife to go out work.
I says, no.
My wife didn't go to work.
I didn't let my wife go.
Yeah, the kids went to school.
Well, it was a Russian school.
It was about 200, Under about 200 people.
No, you couldn't leave.
No, you could go to the village 15, 20 miles and buy something.
We used to take some clothes to the village and get some potatoes, milk, or something else.
As a matter of fact, the commandant had a cow there, just one cow.
Nighttime, I'd pull the milk out.
It was 60 below, 70 below.
Nothing.
Well, they gave us, what do you call it?
Like boots, felt, what they gave us, yes.
You couldn't go in shoes, not at all.
If you go out with shoes you, you don't come back.
Oh, yeah, we worked.
We made to a fire and sit around the fire.
We didn't get much work, you know?
I was working with a horse, pull the timbers out to a river, was in the river.
They didn't get much work with us.
And then when the war started, he says, you free.
The same day, the same hour, it's a fact, I left.
I went, it was to deliver, a deliver was going on a ship and pulling a load.
It was pulling, how you call, a ship with that.
We went in there, and we go to Tomsk.
We was there about 18 months.
No, not special.
If you want to.
You do what you want.
They don't stop you.
No.
If you want to stay home, you stay home.
You don't have to go to work.
Well, you might have to but if you don't go, you don't go.
No.
No, they couldn't.
Yeah, well, we made a strike once.
We made a strike.
We didn't go out to work for three days.
Then they came from Moscow and talked to us.
They're going be good.
They're going to get better livings and so forth, and everybody went back.
They didn't go too hard, but they says, if this would be Russian people doing they would be all shot.
But Polish people they didn't bother too much.
In that there.
Well, this I don't know why.
Well, we was not citizens there.
Yeah.
No.
Well, I received packages.
I don't know until now who sent them to me from Israel.
Two packages I received with everything in the packages, 15-pound packages.
And I don't know who sent it to me until now.
I don't know.
I had relatives, sure, but they didn't send.
When I was in Israel and talked to the relatives, they says they don't know.
But I suppose my brother was alive.
Maybe he paid in and they sent it.
But I didn't saw my brother any more.
When he was back, he wrote me a letter that he's happy when he went back.
But the end was they took him to crematorium.
Him with the whole family because he was with the family.
As a matter of fact, when I came back I find a cousin, a woman.
And she lost her leg.
She was standing-- by the river was eight-story buildings, two of them.
She had a guy working for her with her husband.
And that guy when he was working lived there on that eight-story building.
So he had that woman between two walls stand for three years.
In the nighttime she went down, worked a little and got back there.
And she was, they was shot.
And it hit her leg, took off her leg.
So I met the woman.
She was my cousin, and she says she had a daughter and a son, they went to Russia.
And they-- rather they coming back when I came.
They didn't came when I was there, So she was waiting for them.
She had in San Francisco brothers, two.
She says she's going to San Francisco.
Her name was [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember.
And she was there and waiting until the son and the son was here.
The son was, picture make, like-- Photographer?
Not a photographer.
Handwriting, he made pictures worth millions.
Yeah.
So it was wait-- and I didn't see her any more because we left to a different town.
She was a relative, yeah.
But she had brothers San Francisco.
She says she go to San Francisco.
When I went back from Siberia?
Well, I started working on a truck.
In the warm places, Tashkent.
It's not winter, it's always summer.
Was always with me.
Always with me.
Never left nobody.
I started working on a truck.
Well, the government's truck not my truck.
As a matter of fact, I had to make the truck.
I had to make the motor.
I had to make the chassis.
It took about two months until I made it.
And then I make a living.
I was transferring wheat.
All that I did is transfer wheat.
Nothing.
My wife didn't work never.
Never.
The kids were in school.
When I came back, I think, in '45, I came back to Germany
Huh?
In Tashkent.
Exactly, I was in Tashkent.
We lived in Shymkent but I was in Tashkent May 9, and they announced the war is over.
Oh, there was the people dancing on the roof and whiskey all over.
To go home, to Warsaw.
But we didn't go to Warsaw.
We went to Germany, Liegnitz.
And then from Liegnitz, we stayed not long, two months, I think.
And then we went to Czechoslovakia to Austria and to Germany, Stuttgart.
In Stuttgart, I was living until I went to the States 1945, 1950.
No, I wasn't.
I wasn't going any more to Warsaw.
No, I was once, with my wife, one day and that's it.
We just passed Czechoslovakia.
We didn't stay, we passed.
To Germany.
Through [NON-ENGLISH], no, not to [NON-ENGLISH].
Salzburg.
Yeah, then I started picking up the family from here.
Well, Dave and Maria are supposed to go to Israel.
So they went to our camp and wait now to go to Israel.
And we was, then when we left--
In [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Stuttgart?
No, Liegnitz?
Liegnitz.
Liegnitz.
And when we left and the kids was in a camp.
Then when I left, we go to Germany.
We went in Salzburg.
Then in Salzburg, we decide to pick up the kids not to let them go nowhere.
So I went looking for the kids.
And I go in one town-- I forgot the name-- I go on the street and I see Dave walking.
And then somebody told me they saw Maria.
So I got Dave, and we go look for Maria.
There was a crematorium there by the forest at the end.
And I go out and holler, Maria, Maria!
And she heard me.
[LAUGHS] She came out.
They were sleeping in a-- it was a crematorium.
There was a lot of boys and girls there.
So I got Maria, too.
And a lot of boys and girls-- were their parents was with me in-- in Salzburg.
I went to all of them and went to Salzburg and brought them.
